title: Find shortest path in an unweighted graph (BFS).
description: Find shortest path in an unweighted graph (BFS).

examples.explanation: Start: shortest = inf. DFS(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != dst 3. Neighbors: 1, 2. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. DFS(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != dst. Neighbors: 0,3. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,1,3}, length=2. DFS(3, {0,1,3}, 2): node 3 == dst, update shortest = min(inf,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 3 from visited. Backtrack: remove 1 from visited. Explore neighbor 2: visited = {0,2}, length=1. DFS(2, {0,2}, 1): node 2 != dst. Neighbors: 0,3. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,2,3}, length=2. DFS(3, {0,2,3}, 2): node 3 == dst, update shortest = min(2,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 3, then 2. Return shortest = 2.

Start: shortest = inf. DFS(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != dst 2. Neighbors: 1. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. DFS(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != dst. Neighbors: 0,2. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited = {0,1,2}, length=2. DFS(2, {0,1,2}, 2): node 2 == dst, update shortest = min(inf,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 2, then 1. Return shortest = 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(b^d)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from source to destination using depth-first search, tracking the shortest found. This approach is inefficient for unweighted graphs but demonstrates exhaustive path exploration.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph Using Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DFS exploring all paths to find the shortest path between two nodes in an unweighted graph.

explanation.prose: This problem requires finding the shortest path between a source and destination node in an unweighted graph using a brute force DFS approach. The intuition is that in an unweighted graph, all edges have equal weight, so the shortest path is the one with the fewest edges. While BFS is optimal for this problem, we use DFS to demonstrate exhaustive search by exploring all possible paths and tracking the minimum length. The algorithm works by recursively exploring each neighbor from the current node, maintaining a visited set to avoid cycles within the current path (but allowing revisiting nodes in different paths), and updating the shortest path length when the destination is reached. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize shortest_path_length to infinity. 2. Define a recursive DFS function that takes current node, destination node, visited set for current path, and current path length. 3. If current node equals destination, update shortest_path_length with min of current value and current path length. 4. Otherwise, for each neighbor not in visited set, add neighbor to visited, recursively call DFS with incremented path length, then remove neighbor from visited (backtracking). 5. Start DFS from source with visited containing source and path length 0. 6. Return shortest_path_length (or -1 if still infinity).

connectsTo.reason: This builds on the key subroutine of recursive DFS traversal

timeComplexity.note: where b is branching factor and d is depth, as we explore all possible paths up to depth d

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and visited set

commonMistakes: not backtracking properly (leading to incorrect visited state)
not resetting shortest_path_length for multiple calls
explanation.codePattern: def shortest_path_dfs(graph, src, dst): shortest = float('inf'); def dfs(node, visited, length): nonlocal shortest; if node == dst: shortest = min(shortest, length); return; for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); dfs(neighbor, visited, length + 1); visited.remove(neighbor); dfs(src, {src}, 0); return shortest if shortest != float('inf') else -1

examples.trace: Start: shortest = inf. DFS(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != dst 3. Neighbors: 1, 2. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. DFS(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != dst. Neighbors: 0,3. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,1,3}, length=2. DFS(3, {0,1,3}, 2): node 3 == dst, update shortest = min(inf,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 3 from visited. Backtrack: remove 1 from visited. Explore neighbor 2: visited = {0,2}, length=1. DFS(2, {0,2}, 1): node 2 != dst. Neighbors: 0,3. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,2,3}, length=2. DFS(3, {0,2,3}, 2): node 3 == dst, update shortest = min(2,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 3, then 2. Return shortest = 2.

Start: shortest = inf. DFS(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != dst 2. Neighbors: 1. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. DFS(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != dst. Neighbors: 0,2. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited = {0,1,2}, length=2. DFS(2, {0,1,2}, 2): node 2 == dst, update shortest = min(inf,2)=2. Backtrack: remove 2, then 1. Return shortest = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Traversal with Path Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement DFS that explores all paths from a node while tracking visited nodes and path length.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that performs depth-first search while maintaining state for path exploration. The intuition is to systematically explore each branch deeply before backtracking, keeping track of which nodes are in the current path to avoid cycles, and accumulating the path length. This enables exhaustive search of all possible routes. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define function dfs(node, visited_set, current_length). 2. Base case: if node is destination, process result (e.g., update shortest length). 3. Recursive case: for each neighbor of node not in visited_set, add neighbor to visited_set, call dfs(neighbor, visited_set, current_length + 1), then remove neighbor from visited_set (backtracking). 4. The visited_set prevents revisiting nodes within the same path, allowing exploration of different paths through backtracking. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation depends on graph structure but follows O(b^d) in worst case as it may explore all paths. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of backtracking to explore multiple paths.

connectsTo.reason: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the full solution to explore all paths

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion depth and visited set

commonMistakes: forgetting to backtrack (leading to incorrect visited state)
or using a global visited set incorrectly across different paths
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(node, visited, length): if node == dst: # process; for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); dfs(neighbor, visited, length + 1); visited.remove(neighbor)

examples.trace: Call dfs(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != target 3. Neighbors: 1,2. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. dfs(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != target. Neighbors: 3. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,1,3}, length=2. dfs(3, {0,1,3}, 2): node 3 == target, process (e.g., print path). Backtrack: remove 3. Backtrack: remove 1. Explore neighbor 2: visited = {0,2}, length=1. dfs(2, {0,2}, 1): node 2 != target. Neighbors: 3. neighbor 3 not visited: visited = {0,2,3}, length=2. dfs(3, {0,2,3}, 2): node 3 == target, process. Backtrack: remove 3, then 2.

Call dfs(0, {0}, 0): node 0 != target 2. Neighbors: 1. Explore neighbor 1: visited = {0,1}, length=1. dfs(1, {0,1}, 1): node 1 != target. Neighbors: 2. neighbor 2 not visited: visited = {0,1,2}, length=2. dfs(2, {0,1,2}, 2): node 2 == target, process. Backtrack: remove 2. neighbor 0 in visited, skip. Backtrack: remove 1.

testCases.expected: Reach target at length 2 via two paths

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking to Explore Multiple Paths
ladders.desc: Implement backtracking mechanism to explore all possible paths by modifying and restoring state.

explanation.prose: This level focuses on the backtracking technique essential for exploring all paths in DFS. The intuition is that to examine every possible route from source to destination, we need to try each neighbor at every step, but after exploring one path, we must revert changes (like visited nodes) to try alternative paths. Backtracking does this by adding a node to the current path state before recursion and removing it afterward. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Maintain a mutable state (e.g., set of visited nodes in current path). 2. Before recursing to a neighbor, update state to include that neighbor. 3. After the recursive call returns, restore state by removing the neighbor. 4. This allows the same node to be visited in different paths at different times. Time complexity involves state modifications O(1) per step, but overall contributes to O(b^d) exploration. Connection to L3: It relies on building blocks like set operations for state management.

connectsTo.reason: This logic is embedded in the DFS subroutine where visited set is modified and restored

examples.input: State: visited set = {0}, current node = 0, neighbors = [1, 2]
examples.output: Explore path via 1, then backtrack and explore via 2
examples.trace: Initial visited = {0}. For neighbor 1: visited.add(1) -> visited = {0,1}. Recurse (explore path through 1). After return: visited.remove(1) -> visited = {0}. For neighbor 2: visited.add(2) -> visited = {0,2}. Recurse (explore path through 2). After return: visited.remove(2) -> visited = {0}.

examples.input: State: visited set = {A}, current node = A, neighbors = [B, C, D]
examples.output: Explore paths via B, C, D sequentially with backtracking
examples.trace: Initial visited = {A}. neighbor B: visited.add(B) -> {A,B}, recurse, then visited.remove(B) -> {A}. neighbor C: visited.add(C) -> {A,C}, recurse, then visited.remove(C) -> {A}. neighbor D: visited.add(D) -> {A,D}, recurse, then visited.remove(D) -> {A}.

testCases.input: visited = {X}, neighbors = [Y, Z, W], perform backtracking
visited = {a}, neighbors = [b, c, d, e], perform backtracking
visited = {root}, neighbors = [child1, child2, child3], perform backtracking

ladders.title: Building Block — Maintaining Visited Set for Current Path
ladders.desc: Create and manipulate a set to track nodes visited in the current path to prevent cycles.

explanation.prose: This building block involves using a set data structure to keep track of nodes that have been visited in the current path during DFS traversal. The intuition is that in graph traversal, especially when exploring all paths, we must avoid cycles within a single path (where a node is visited multiple times in the same path), but allow nodes to be revisited in different paths. A set provides O(1) average-time operations for addition, removal, and membership checks, making it efficient for this purpose. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty set at the start of path exploration. 2. When moving to a new node, add it to the set if not already present. 3. When backtracking from a node, remove it from the set. 4. Check set membership before exploring neighbors to decide if a node can be visited. Time complexity for set operations is O(1) on average. Connection to L4: It relies on basic set operations like add and remove.

connectsTo.reason: This set is the state managed during backtracking

commonMistakes: using a list instead of set (leading to O(n) checks)
or incorrectly sharing set across different paths

examples.input: Start with visited = {0}. Visit node 1, then backtrack.
examples.trace: Initial visited = {0}. Add node 1: visited.add(1) -> visited = {0,1}. Backtrack: visited.remove(1) -> visited = {0}.

examples.input: Start with visited = {A}. Visit B, then C, then backtrack C, then B.
examples.trace: Initial visited = {A}. Add B: visited = {A,B}. Add C: visited = {A,B,C}. Remove C: visited = {A,B}. Remove B: visited = {A}.

testCases.input: visited = {a, b}, add c, remove b
visited = {X}, add Y, add Z, remove Y
visited = {start}, add mid, add end, remove mid
visited = {A}, check if B in visited
visited = {root}, add child1, add child2, remove child1, add child3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Set Addition and Removal
ladders.desc: Perform add and remove operations on a set data structure.

explanation.prose: This level covers the fundamental operations of adding an element to a set and removing an element from a set. The intuition is that sets are unordered collections of unique elements, and these operations allow dynamic modification of the set's contents. Adding an element inserts it if not already present; removing an element deletes it if present (or does nothing if absent). These are essential for managing state like visited nodes. The step-by-step algorithm: For add: 1. Check if element is already in set (optional, as set handles uniqueness). 2. Insert element into set. For remove: 1. Check if element is in set. 2. If present, delete it; else, no operation or handle error. Space complexity may increase with add if new element is added. Connection to L5: Sets are built on foundational concepts like hash tables.

connectsTo.reason: These operations are used to maintain the visited set

commonMistakes: or not handling missing elements in remove

examples.trace: Start s = {1,2}. Add 3: s.add(3) -> s = {1,2,3}. Remove 2: s.remove(2) -> s = {1,3}.

examples.input: Set s = {'a'}. Add 'b', add 'a', remove 'c', remove 'a'.
examples.trace: Start s = {'a'}. Add 'b': s = {'a','b'}. Add 'a': s remains {'a','b'} (duplicate not added). Remove 'c': no change (or error if checked). Remove 'a': s = {'b'}.

testCases.input: s = {alpha, beta}, add gamma, remove alpha

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sets as Collections of Unique Elements
ladders.desc: Learn what a set is and how it stores unique elements without order.

explanation.prose: This foundational level introduces the set data structure, which is a collection of unique elements with no defined order. The intuition is that sets are useful when you need to track membership (whether an item is present) without caring about duplicates or sequence. In Python and similar languages, sets are typically implemented using hash tables, allowing fast lookups. Key properties: elements are unique (adding a duplicate has no effect), unordered (no indexing), and mutable (can add/remove). Common operations include creation, adding elements, removing elements, and checking membership. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) on average due to hashing. Space complexity is proportional to number of elements. Connection to broader context: Sets are a basic data structure in programming.

connectsTo.reason: Sets are used for the add/remove operations

explanation.codePattern: s = set() creates empty set; s = {1,2,3} creates with elements; if x in s checks membership

examples.input: Create a set with elements 1, 2, 3. Check if 2 is in set. Add 4. Remove 2.
examples.trace: Create set: s = {1,2,3}. Check 2 in s: True. Add 4: s.add(4) -> s = {1,2,3,4}. Remove 2: s.remove(2) -> s = {1,3,4}.

examples.input: Create empty set. Add 'apple', add 'banana', add 'apple'. Check size.
examples.trace: Create: s = set(). Add 'apple': s = {'apple'}. Add 'banana': s = {'apple','banana'}. Add 'apple' again: no change (duplicate). Size: len(s) = 2.

testCases.input: s = {5}, add 6, check if 5 in s
s = {p,q,r}, check if q in s
s = {item}, remove item, check if empty

approaches.name: Standard BFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search to explore the graph level by level from the source, guaranteeing the first time destination is reached is via the shortest path. This is the classic solution for unweighted graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph Using BFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of BFS to find shortest path between two nodes in an unweighted graph.

explanation.prose: This is the complete solution for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph using BFS. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level from the source, guaranteeing that the first time we reach the destination, we've found the shortest path (minimum number of edges). This works because in unweighted graphs, all edges have equal weight, so the path with fewest edges is optimal. The algorithm maintains a queue for BFS traversal, a visited set to avoid cycles, and a parent dictionary to reconstruct the path. We start from the source node, explore all its neighbors, then their neighbors, and so on. When we reach the destination, we backtrack using parent pointers to reconstruct the shortest path.

connectsTo.reason: as it uses the BFS traversal subroutine to explore the graph systematically

timeComplexity.note: where V is vertices and E is edges, as each vertex and edge is processed once

spaceComplexity.note: for the queue, visited set, and parent dictionary

commonMistakes: not marking nodes as visited before enqueuing (causing infinite loops)
explanation.codePattern: Initialize queue with source, mark visited, track parent

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue = [0], visited = {0}, parent = {0: None}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbors: 1 (not visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, set parent: {0:None,1:0,2:0}, enqueue: [1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 3 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2,3}, set parent: {3:1}, enqueue: [2,3]. Step 4: Dequeue 2. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 3 (visited). Step 5: Dequeue 3. Current = destination. Reconstruct path: 3 → parent 1 → parent 0 → None. Reverse: [0,1,3].

Step 1: queue = [0], visited = {0}, parent = {0:None}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbor: 1 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1}, parent: {1:0}, enqueue: [1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, parent: {2:1}, enqueue: [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2. Neighbors: 1 (visited), 3 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2,3}, parent: {3:2}, enqueue: [3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3. Destination reached. Reconstruct: 3→2→1→0→None. Reverse: [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Path Tracking
ladders.desc: BFS traversal that tracks parent pointers to enable path reconstruction.

explanation.prose: This subroutine performs BFS traversal while maintaining parent pointers for each visited node. The intuition is that BFS naturally explores nodes in increasing order of distance from the source, and by recording the parent (the node we came from), we can later reconstruct the path. The algorithm uses a queue to process nodes in FIFO order, a visited set to prevent revisiting, and a parent dictionary mapping each node to its predecessor in the BFS tree. We start with the source node, mark it visited with no parent, and enqueue it. While the queue isn't empty, we dequeue a node, explore its neighbors, and for each unvisited neighbor, mark it visited, set its parent to the current node, and enqueue it. This creates a BFS tree rooted at the source. This feeds into L0 by providing the traversal mechanism; L0 adds destination checking and path reconstruction.

timeComplexity.note: as we process each node and edge once

commonMistakes: not using a queue (using stack gives DFS)
forgetting to mark visited before enqueuing (causing duplicates)

examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [0], visited = {0}, parent = {0:None}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbors: 1 (not visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, set parent: {1:0,2:0}, enqueue: [1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited). Step 4: Dequeue 2. Neighbors: 0 (visited). Queue empty. Parent dictionary: {0:None,1:0,2:0}.

Step 1: queue = [0], visited = {0}, parent = {0:None}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbor: 1 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1}, parent: {1:0}, enqueue: [1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, parent: {2:1}, enqueue: [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2. Neighbors: 1 (visited). Queue empty. Parent: {0:None,1:0,2:1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Level-by-Level Exploration with Queue
ladders.desc: Using a queue to explore nodes in order of increasing distance from source.

explanation.prose: This core logic implements the fundamental BFS mechanism: exploring nodes level by level using a queue. The intuition is that in unweighted graphs, the shortest path corresponds to the minimum number of edges, and BFS processes nodes in increasing order of edge count from the source. By using a FIFO queue, we ensure all nodes at distance d are processed before nodes at distance d+1. The algorithm starts with the source node in the queue. While the queue isn't empty, we dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This guarantees that when we first encounter a node, we've reached it via the shortest path. This feeds into L1 by providing the traversal order; L1 adds parent tracking.

timeComplexity.note: as each node enters the queue once and each edge is examined once

spaceComplexity.note: for the queue and visited set

commonMistakes: not using a visited set (causing infinite loops)

examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [0], visited = {0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, visit 0. Neighbors: 1 (not visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, enqueue: [1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, visit 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited). Step 4: Dequeue 2, visit 2. Neighbors: 0 (visited). Queue empty. Visited order: 0,1,2.

Step 1: queue = [0], visited = {0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, visit 0. Neighbor: 1 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1}, enqueue: [1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, visit 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 2 (not visited). Mark visited: {0,1,2}, enqueue: [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, visit 2. Neighbors: 1 (visited). Queue empty. Visited order: 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Neighbor Iteration
ladders.desc: Representing a graph and iterating through neighbors of a node.

explanation.prose: This building block involves representing an unweighted graph and accessing neighbors of a given node. The intuition is that BFS requires efficiently accessing all adjacent nodes from a current node to explore them. Typically, graphs are represented using adjacency lists (dictionary mapping nodes to lists of neighbors) for O(1) neighbor access. The algorithm involves defining the graph structure and implementing a function to get neighbors. For a given node, we retrieve its list of adjacent nodes from the adjacency list. This is fundamental because BFS processes each node and examines all its edges. Time complexity for neighbor iteration is O(degree(node)), where degree is the number of edges incident to the node. This feeds into L2 by providing the graph traversal mechanism; L2 uses this to explore neighbors during BFS.
commonMistakes: using adjacency matrix (wastes space for sparse graphs)

examples.trace: Graph is represented as dictionary. For node 0, graph[0] returns the list [1,2]. These are the neighbors of node 0.
For node 1, graph[1] returns [0,2]. These are the neighbors: node 0 and node 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management (FIFO)
ladders.desc: Implementing FIFO queue operations: enqueue and dequeue.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves implementing a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue, which is essential for BFS. The intuition is that BFS requires processing nodes in the order they are discovered, and a queue ensures that nodes at the same distance from the source are processed before nodes further away. The queue supports two main operations: enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front). We can implement this using a list in Python (with append for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue) or collections.deque for efficiency. The algorithm involves initializing an empty queue, then repeatedly enqueuing new nodes and dequeuing nodes to process. Time complexity for enqueue and dequeue is O(1) with proper implementation. This feeds into L3 by providing the data structure for level-by-level traversal; L3 uses the queue in BFS.
commonMistakes: inefficient dequeue from list front (O(n) if not using deque)

examples.input: Operations: enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [], enqueue 1 → queue = [1]. Step 2: enqueue 2 → queue = [1,2]. Step 3: dequeue → remove front 1, queue = [2], output 1. Step 4: enqueue 3 → queue = [2,3]. Step 5: dequeue → remove front 2, queue = [3], output 2. Step 6: dequeue → remove front 3, queue = [], output 3.

examples.input: Operations: enqueue A, enqueue B, dequeue, enqueue C, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [], enqueue A → queue = [A]. Step 2: enqueue B → queue = [A,B]. Step 3: dequeue → remove front A, queue = [B], output A. Step 4: enqueue C → queue = [B,C]. Step 5: dequeue → remove front B, queue = [C], output B.

testCases.input: Operations: enqueue A, dequeue, enqueue B, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 5, enqueue 6, enqueue 7, dequeue, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
Operations: enqueue a, enqueue b, enqueue c, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Operations: enqueue p, enqueue q, dequeue, enqueue r, dequeue, enqueue s, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 3, enqueue 4, dequeue, dequeue, enqueue 5, dequeue
Operations: enqueue m, dequeue, enqueue n, dequeue, enqueue o, dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Nodes and Edges in Graphs
ladders.desc: Understanding basic graph components: nodes (vertices) and edges.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept introduces the basic elements of a graph: nodes (also called vertices) and edges. The intuition is that graphs model relationships between objects, where nodes represent entities and edges represent connections between them. In an unweighted graph, edges have no associated weights, meaning all connections are treated equally. A graph can be directed or undirected; for BFS shortest path, we typically use undirected graphs where edges are bidirectional. Nodes are usually represented by integers or labels, and edges are pairs of nodes. For example, in a social network, nodes are people and edges are friendships. This is fundamental because BFS operates on this structure: it starts at a source node and follows edges to explore the graph. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. This feeds into L4 by providing the data that the queue manages; L4 uses nodes in queue operations.
explanation.codePattern: Nodes: 0,1,2; Edges: (0,1), (0,2) meaning connections between nodes

examples.trace: The graph has three nodes: 0, 1, and 2. There are two edges: one between node 0 and node 1, and one between node 0 and node 2. This means node 0 is connected to both node 1 and node 2, but nodes 1 and 2 are not directly connected to each other.

examples.input: Graph with nodes: A,B,C and edges: (A,B), (B,C)
examples.output: Nodes: A,B,C; Edges: A connected to B, B connected to C
examples.trace: Nodes are A, B, C. Edges: A-B and B-C. This means A is connected to B, and B is connected to C. A is not directly connected to C, but there's a path A-B-C.

testCases.expected: Nodes: a,b,c,d; Edges: a-b, b-c, c-d
Nodes: M,N,O; Edges: M-N, N-O, O-M

approaches.name: Bidirectional BFS O(b^(d/2))
approaches.summary: Run BFS simultaneously from both source and destination, meeting in the middle to reduce search space. This is optimal for large graphs where the branching factor is high.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bidirectional BFS for Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of bidirectional BFS to find shortest path between source and destination in an unweighted graph.

explanation.prose: Bidirectional BFS simultaneously explores from both source and destination nodes, meeting in the middle to dramatically reduce search space compared to traditional BFS. This is particularly effective for large graphs with high branching factors. The intuition is that searching from both ends reduces the exponential growth frontier from b^d to approximately 2b^(d/2), where b is branching factor and d is shortest path length. We maintain two queues and two visited dictionaries (with path tracking). When frontiers intersect (a node appears in both visited sets), we reconstruct the complete path by combining paths from both sides. The algorithm terminates when intersection is found or both queues are exhausted (no path exists).

connectsTo.reason: This integrates the bidirectional expansion logic with path reconstruction

timeComplexity.note: ) and space complexity is O(b^(d/2)), significantly better than traditional BFS's O(b^d) for large d

commonMistakes: not checking for intersection at each expansion step (causing missed early termination)
incorrect path reconstruction when meeting node is expanded from both sides simultaneously
forgetting to handle cases where source equals destination
explanation.codePattern: Initialize two queues with source/destination, two visited dicts mapping node->[path], alternate expansions checking for intersection, reconstruct path when found

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize forward queue=[0], forward_visited={0:[0]}, backward queue=[5], backward_visited={5:[5]}
Step 2: Expand forward from 0: neighbors 1,2. Add to forward_visited: {0:[0], 1:[0,1], 2:[0,2]}. Check intersection: 1,2 not in backward_visited.
Step 3: Expand backward from 5: neighbors 3,4. Add to backward_visited: {5:[5], 3:[5,3], 4:[5,4]}. Check intersection: 3,4 not in forward_visited.
Step 4: Expand forward from 1: neighbor 3. Add {3:[0,1,3]}. Check intersection: 3 now in backward_visited! Intersection found at node 3.
Step 5: Reconstruct path: forward path to 3=[0,1,3], backward path to 3=[5,3]. Reverse backward path=[3,5]. Combine: [0,1,3] + [5] (skip duplicate 3) = [0,1,3,5].
Step 6: Alternative shorter path check: Continue expansion might find shorter. Expand forward from 2: neighbor 4. Add {4:[0,2,4]}. Check intersection: 4 in backward_visited! Path: [0,2,4] + [5] = [0,2,4,5] (length 3 vs previous 4). Return shortest: [0,2,4,5].

Step 1: forward_queue=[0], forward_visited={0:[0]}, backward_queue=[3], backward_visited={3:[3]}
Step 2: Expand forward: 0->1, forward_visited={0:[0],1:[0,1]}. No intersection.
Step 3: Expand backward: 3->2, backward_visited={3:[3],2:[3,2]}. No intersection.
Step 4: Expand forward: 1->2, forward_visited adds {2:[0,1,2]}. Check: 2 in backward_visited! Intersection found.
Step 5: Reconstruct: forward path [0,1,2], backward path [3,2], reversed [2,3]. Combine: [0,1,2] + [3] = [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bidirectional Expansion with Intersection Detection
ladders.desc: Core bidirectional expansion logic that alternates between forward and backward BFS levels while checking for meeting point.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine implements the alternating expansion mechanism that makes bidirectional BFS efficient. The intuition is that by expanding one level from each side alternately, we ensure balanced progress toward the middle while minimizing memory usage. We maintain two queues (forward and backward) and two visited dictionaries that track which nodes have been reached from each direction. At each expansion step, we process all nodes at the current frontier level from one direction, add their unvisited neighbors to the queue and visited dict, then immediately check if any newly added node exists in the opposite visited dictionary. If found, we've detected an intersection and can terminate early. The alternating pattern (forward, then backward, then forward, etc.) ensures we don't favor one direction, which is crucial for optimal b^(d/2) complexity. Time complexity per expansion step is O(b) where b is branching factor at that level.

connectsTo.reason: This is the main loop of the full algorithm - once intersection is detected, L0 handles path reconstruction

spaceComplexity.value: O(number of visited nodes from both sides)

commonMistakes: expanding multiple levels from one side before alternating (defeating the bidirectional benefit)
not checking for intersection after each complete level expansion
inefficient intersection checking (should use dictionary lookups
explanation.codePattern: While both queues non-empty: expand all nodes in current forward queue level, check intersection, if found return; then expand all nodes in current backward queue level, check intersection, if found return

examples.trace:
Step 1: Expand forward from queue [0]: process node 0, add neighbors 1,2 to forward_queue. forward_visited={0,1,2}. Check intersection: 1,2 not in backward_visited={3}.
Step 2: Expand backward from queue [3]: process node 3, add neighbors 1,2 to backward_queue. backward_visited={3,1,2}. Check intersection: 1 is now in forward_visited! Intersection detected at node 1.
Total expansions: 2 (one forward level, one backward level).
